stops.name: Circular Quay
stops.shortDescription: The bustling main ferry transport hub situated on the edge of Sydney Cove. It is the perfect vantage point to view both the Opera House and the Harbour Bridge simultaneously.

stops.name: Sydney Opera House
stops.shortDescription: A masterpiece of 20th-century architecture designed by Danish architect Jørn Utzon. Its famous gleaming white 'sails' are actually covered with over one million ceramic tiles.

stops.name: Royal Botanic Garden
stops.shortDescription: Established in 1816, making it the oldest scientific institution in Australia. This lush, 74-acre waterfront oasis is home to thousands of native plant species and cheeky sulphur-crested cockatoos.

stops.name: Customs House
stops.shortDescription: Built in 1845 to collect taxes on imported goods entering the colony. Today, its impressive glass floor reveals a spectacular, highly detailed scale model of downtown Sydney.

stops.name: The Rocks Discovery Museum
stops.shortDescription: Housed in a restored 1850s sandstone warehouse, this free museum tells the story of The Rocks area. It traces the area's history from pre-European aboriginal days to the present.

stops.name: Sydney Harbour Bridge Pylon Lookout
stops.shortDescription: Affectionately known as 'The Coathanger', it is the largest steel arch bridge in the world. The South East Pylon houses a museum detailing its dangerous construction and offers a breathtaking open-air viewing deck.

stops.name: Dawes Point Park
stops.shortDescription: Located directly underneath the massive steel span of the Harbour Bridge. It features the historic remains of a 1790 artillery battery originally built to defend Sydney Cove from naval attacks.

stops.name: Campbell's Cove
stops.shortDescription: A picturesque waterfront promenade lined with beautifully restored 19th-century sandstone storehouses. It provides one of the absolute best, unobstructed photo angles of the Sydney Opera House.

stops.name: Cadmans Cottage
stops.shortDescription: Built in 1816, this is one of only a handful of Sydney buildings that survive from the first 30 years of the colony. It was originally built right on the water's edge before land reclamation.

stops.name: The Argyle Cut
stops.shortDescription: A monumental tunnel connecting The Rocks to Millers Point. It was brutally hand-hewn through solid sandstone by convict labor in the 1840s using only picks, crowbars, and eventual explosives.

stops.name: Observatory Hill
stops.shortDescription: A high, grassy vantage point offering sweeping panoramic views over the western harbor. It is crowned by the historic, copper-domed Sydney Observatory built in 1858.

stops.name: Barangaroo Reserve
stops.shortDescription: A spectacular, award-winning waterfront park that recreated the natural, pre-colonial headland. It features 75,000 native Australian plants and beautiful terraced sandstone blocks plunging into the water.

stops.name: Barangaroo South
stops.shortDescription: Sydney's newest and sleekest dining and commercial precinct. It is dramatically anchored by the twisting, petal-shaped glass skyscraper of the ultra-luxurious Crown Sydney.

stops.name: King Street Wharf
stops.shortDescription: A vibrant, bustling waterfront destination packed with alfresco restaurants and bars. It serves as a fantastic final stop to grab a drink and watch the sun set over Darling Harbour.
